content.text: Slash commands provide a way to control Claude Code sessions with special commands that start with

. They can perform actions like clearing conversation history, compacting messages, or getting help. Unlike freeform prompts, built-in commands trigger fixed runtime logic rather than invoking the language model—they are deterministic operations wired directly into Claude Code's internals.
Built-in commands are shortcuts for common actions. There are 55+ built-in commands and 5 bundled skills available. Type
in Claude Code to see the full list, or type
followed by any letters to filter.
There are four distinct categories of slash commands that can appear when you type
Built-in commands, provided by Claude Code itself (like
); Skills, which are user-defined commands created as
files; Plugin commands, from installed plugins; and MCP prompts, which are commands from connected MCP servers.
This article covers only the built-in category—the commands that ship with Claude Code and require no configuration.
The built-in commands serve a function that natural language prompts cannot reliably fill. Telling Claude "please forget everything and start over" is a suggestion;
is a guarantee. This distinction matters enormously for agentic workflows where context budget, permissions, and session continuity are first-class concerns.
Context management commands directly control how many tokens remain available, which determines how long a session can remain coherent.
Permission commands set the trust boundary between Claude and the host system in a structured, auditable way.
Session management commands allow work to pause, resume, branch, and replay—treating a coding session as a durable object rather than a throwaway conversation.
Diagnostic commands give objective readings of internal state (token usage, installation health, configuration) that would otherwise require inspecting internal files.
Claude Code's slash commands are the control plane for agentic development: they set trust (
When a Claude Code session initializes, the runtime produces a

message that lists every slash command available in that environment. Only commands that work without an interactive terminal are dispatchable through the SDK; the system/init message lists the ones available in your session.
When building automation with the Agent SDK, this is how to enumerate the available commands programmatically:
In the interactive CLI, simply typing
opens the autocomplete menu showing every available command, including custom skills and MCP prompts alongside built-ins.

The following sections cover the most important built-in commands grouped by function. The full set exceeds 55 entries; the ones below are those most relevant to day-to-day use and to understanding the system.
is the token lifesaver. When a long session fills the context window,
compresses the conversation history into a dense summary. You can pass instructions to steer what it retains. For example:
/compact Focus on the auth module changes and current failing tests

Without arguments, Claude Code decides what to retain. With an argument, the summary prioritizes the topics named. The conversation history is replaced with the condensed version; subsequent turns continue from the summary rather than the original exchange.
This is a runtime operation, not a model-generated response—the compaction itself happens deterministically, though the summary content is model-generated.
replaces conversation history with a compressed summary, preserving the thread of what was discussed. Use
when you want Claude to remember context from earlier in the session but need to free up token budget.

command resets the conversation to an empty context, so subsequent prompts start with no prior conversation history. File edits made during the session are preserved on disk—only the in-memory conversation is wiped.
when you are switching to a completely different task and do not need any prior context. Keyboard shortcut
Input history resets when you run
to start a new session. The previous session's conversation is preserved and can be resumed.
visualizes context usage as a colored grid. This gives an at-a-glance token budget reading without leaving the session. Knowing the proportion consumed helps decide whether to
proactively before hitting the ceiling mid-task.
editing interface for adding, updating, or removing project rules. This is the in-session way to manage the persistent memory layer—equivalent to opening
in a text editor, but integrated into the Claude Code interface.
Picks up a previously saved session. Without arguments, it opens an interactive session picker. With a session name or identifier, it jumps directly to that conversation. The CLI equivalent is
Branches a conversation into a new session (alias:

). This is how to explore a risky approach without permanently committing the current session's state. The fork shares the context at the point of branching but diverges from there. Useful when trying an uncertain refactoring strategy: fork, try it, and discard the branch if it fails rather than rewinding the main session.
). Claude Code automatically creates checkpoints before tool use.
navigates to a previous checkpoint, undoing both the conversation and any file changes made since that point. This is a more granular escape hatch than
, which only affects the conversation, not disk state.

Renames the current session. Without an argument, Claude Code auto-generates a name from conversation context. With an argument, it sets the name explicitly. Named sessions are resumable by name rather than by timestamp, which matters when running parallel sessions across multiple tasks or branches.
exports the current conversation to a file or clipboard. Useful for sharing session transcripts, creating documentation from a work session, or archiving reasoning for later review.
analyzes your project and generates a

file—the configuration that persists project-specific rules, conventions, and context across all sessions. Without it, you re-explain your project's conventions every time. With it, Claude always knows your stack, testing framework, and coding standards.
environment variable set triggers an interactive flow that walks through skills, hooks, and personal memory file setup as well.
control which tools Claude can use and what file paths are permitted. This is the in-session interface for the same permission layer configurable in
. Viewing current permissions is safe; modifying them takes effect immediately for the rest of the session.

manages automation that triggers on tool events: before file edits, after bash commands, on session start. The critical difference between hooks and prompts: hooks guarantee execution, prompts merely suggest it.
command opens an interactive menu for configuring hook definitions, which is often more ergonomic than editing the JSON directly.

manages MCP servers and OAuth. This is where connected MCP servers are inspected, added, or removed. Once an MCP server is connected, its tools and prompts become available to Claude in that session, and its prompts appear in the

selects the model with left/right arrows for effort. In the interactive interface, this opens a picker for switching between available models (Sonnet, Haiku, Opus) mid-session. Changes take effect for the next turn. This allows starting a session on a faster, cheaper model and switching to a more capable one only when the task demands deeper reasoning.
requires Opus 4.6. Effort levels range from
, controlling how much internal reasoning the model applies to each turn. Lower effort is faster and cheaper; higher effort produces more thorough responses at greater cost. The
level lets the model decide based on the complexity it perceives.
Toggles fast mode. Fast mode prioritizes response latency over thoroughness, useful when doing rapid iteration where speed of feedback matters more than depth.

toggles plan permission mode. In this mode, Claude proposes each tool action and waits for your approval before executing it. Use it when working in an unfamiliar codebase or before a large refactor you want to review step by step.
/plan refactor the auth flow without changing behavior
), Claude first maps out a structured plan before proposing any tool calls. This is the safest way to begin a large task: see the full scope of proposed changes before a single file is touched.

diagnoses installation health. It checks environment variables, Node.js version compatibility, authentication status, network connectivity to the Anthropic API, and whether any configuration files are malformed. Run this after installation, after upgrading, or whenever Claude Code behaves unexpectedly.
shows version, model, and account. More specifically, it displays the active model, permission mode, connected MCP servers, and overall configuration state. Running
at the start of a session confirms settings before committing to a long task.

shows token usage statistics. For API users paying per token, this tracks spend in real time across the session. A single agentic loop can consume significantly more tokens than a simple chat exchange, and
builds intuition for which operations are expensive.

visualizes daily usage, sessions, and streaks. This is the longer-term view for Pro and Max subscribers—a graphical display of usage patterns across sessions, days, and streaks, useful for monitoring consumption against plan limits.
Lists all available commands, including custom skills and MCP prompts alongside built-ins. The description field of each custom command appears here, making
the integrated reference for the current session's full command vocabulary.

analyzes the branch for security vulnerabilities. This is a bundled skill that runs a structured security analysis over the current diff or codebase, checking for OWASP Top 10 vulnerabilities, exposed credentials, injection risks, and insecure configurations. It applies plan mode automatically, so findings are presented before any remediation is proposed.

After you run it, Claude will automatically review your PRs. This is actually useful because as you use more AI tools, your PR volume increases. This command sets up the GitHub Actions integration that runs automated code review on pull requests.
Claude Code includes a set of bundled skills that are available in every session unless disabled with the
. Unlike most built-in commands, which execute fixed logic directly, bundled skills are prompt-based: they give Claude detailed instructions and let it orchestrate the work using its tools.
The distinction matters: pure built-in commands (like
) are hardwired runtime operations that bypass the model entirely. Bundled skills (like
) invoke the model with a structured system prompt and therefore consume tokens and agentic turns.
Key bundled skills available as slash commands:
— structured diff review at a chosen effort level, checking for correctness bugs, security issues, and test coverage
— orchestrates large-scale parallel changes using worktrees, useful for cross-cutting refactors
— systematic debugging with structured diagnosis steps
— repeatedly executes a task on a schedule within the session (e.g.,
A few built-in commands are also available through the Skill tool, including

are not. The distinction between which built-ins can be model-invoked and which cannot is a deliberate design choice: session state operations like compaction and clearing are too impactful to be triggered autonomously by the model.
How Commands Work in the SDK

When using the Claude Agent SDK to build programmatic pipelines, slash commands are sent as ordinary prompt strings. Send slash commands by including them in your prompt string, just like regular text.
in the SDK requires Claude Code v2.1.117 or later. In earlier versions it is omitted from
message before dispatching a command programmatically.
A deliberate session opening looks like this:

/status          # Confirm model, account, and permission mode
/context         # Check if prior context is loaded (after a resume)
/plan <goal>     # Scope the task before touching files
For long-running tasks, proactive context management prevents the abrupt stall that happens when the window fills:

/context         # Check remaining budget (do this periodically)
/compact Focus on the payment integration work and open test failures
/context         # Confirm headroom restored
when context usage exceeds 80%, or whenever you see the "context is getting large" warning.

/clear           # Wipe context before an unrelated task
/init            # If switching to a new project
/permissions     # Verify trust boundaries for the new context

often. Every time you start something new, clear the chat. You don't need all that history eating your tokens, and you definitely don't need Claude running compaction calls to summarize old conversations.

/branch experiment-risky-refactor   # Fork before trying uncertain approach
# ... try the refactor ...
# If it fails: close the session and resume the original
# If it works: merge the learnings back
destroys all context, including the understanding Claude has built about the codebase. If the goal is to free up token budget while preserving the thread of work,
By the time Claude Code auto-compacts, the quality of the summary is constrained by what fits. Manually running
with specific instructions while still well within the budget produces better summaries.

The default mode allows Claude to start making changes immediately. For any refactor touching more than a handful of files, entering plan mode first—so every proposed change is visible before it lands—avoids costly rollbacks.
proactively after any upgrade is a good habit. Configuration issues introduced by an update are much easier to diagnose before they cause mysterious failures mid-session.
does not change the default model for future sessions. To persist the model preference, set it in
in the SDK init message before dispatching.
Commands whose prerequisites are not met—such as
on older versions—simply do not appear in the list. Dispatching them regardless results in the string being interpreted as a natural language prompt rather than a command.
as routine maintenance, not emergency surgery.

Use it when context usage exceeds 80%, or whenever you see the "context is getting large" warning. Passing a brief instruction about what to prioritize yields meaningfully better summaries than running it without arguments.
Name sessions immediately when the task is non-trivial.
resumes reliably. Unnamed sessions appear as timestamps in the picker, which is difficult to navigate after a few days.
The few seconds it takes to review a proposed plan before execution is almost always faster than reverting unintended changes after the fact.
Switching to a more capable model without increasing effort may not produce noticeably better results. For complex reasoning tasks,
effort is the combination that actually leverages the model's full capability.
For scripted/CI usage, check available commands dynamically.
The set of built-in commands evolves across Claude Code releases. Rather than hardcoding which commands to use, read the

The clearest way to think about built-in slash commands is as the difference between talking to a system and commanding it. Natural language prompts are inputs to the language model—they go through inference, may be misinterpreted, consume tokens, and produce probabilistic outputs. Built-in slash commands bypass the model entirely and operate on the session runtime directly, like API calls to a backend.

This creates a clean separation in Claude Code's architecture: the conversational interface for open-ended work, and the command interface for deterministic session control. Effective use of Claude Code means knowing which layer to reach for. Context is filling up?
—not "please summarize what we've done". Trying a risky approach?
—not "let's try something, I might want to go back". Starting fresh on a new project?

to discover what your build exposes—availability varies by version, plan, and platform. Treat the official Commands page as source of truth. The full canonical reference for all built-in commands is maintained at
and reflects the current Claude Code release.
